title: Electric Field and Electric Field Lines

concepts.title: Comparison Between Electric and Gravitational Force
concepts.conceptBody:
Comparison Between Electric and Gravitational Force 

Electrical force and gravitational force are the two non-contact forces. Coulomb's law equation for electrical force bears a strong resemblance to Newton's equation for universal gravitation.

$\begin{matrix} {{\text{F}}_{\text{elect }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{\text{k}{{\text{Q}}_{1}}{{\text{Q}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{d}}^{2}}} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{F}}_{\text{grav }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\frac{\text{G}{{\text{m}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{d}}^{2}}} \\ \left( \text{k}=9\times {{10}^{9}}\text{N}{{\text{m}}^{2}}/{{\text{C}}^{2}} \right) & \left( \text{G}=6.67\times {{10}^{-11}}\text{N}{{\text{m}}^{2}}/\text{k}{{\text{g}}^{2}} \right) \\ \end{matrix}$

The two equations have a very similar form. Both equations show an inverse square relationship between force and separation distance. And both equations show that the force is proportional to the product of the quantity that causes the force-charge in the case of electrical force and mass in the case of gravitational force. Yet there are some striking differences between these two forces. First, a comparison of the proportionality constants - k versus $\text{G}$ - reveals that the Coulomb's law constant (k) is significantly greater than Newton's universal gravitation constant (G). Subsequently a unit of charge will attract a unit of charge with significantly more force than a unit of mass will attract a unit of mass. Second, gravitational forces are only attractive; electrical forces can be either attractive or repulsive.

The inverse square relationship between force and distance which is woven into the equation is common to both non-contact forces. This relationship highlights the importance of separation distance when it comes to the electrical force between charged objects.

concepts.title: Electric Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Field

A charged particle cannot directly interact with another particle kept at a distance. A charge produces something called an electric field in the space found it and this electric field exerts a force on any other charge (except the source charge itself) placed in it.

Thus, the region surrounding a charge or distribution of charge in which its electrical effects can be observed is called the electric field of the charge or distribution of charge. Electric field at a point can be defined that all these are functions of positions $\vec{r}\left( \text{x},\text{y},\text{z} \right)$. The field propagates through space with the speed of light, c. Thus, if a charge is suddenly moved, the force it exerts on another charge a distance $r$ away does not change until a time r/c later.

concepts.title: Electric Field Strength
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Field Strength

Like its gravitational counterpart, the electric field strength (often called electric field) at a point in an electric field is defined as the electrostatic force ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{e}}}$ per unit positive charge. Thus, if the electrostatic force experienced by a small test charge ${{q}_{0}}$ is ${{\vec{F}}_{e}}$, then field strength at that point is defined as,

$\vec{E}=\underset{{{\text{q}}_{0}}\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{{{{\vec{F}}}_{\text{e}}}}{{{\text{q}}_{0}}}$

The electric field is a vector quantity and its direction is the same as the direction of the force ${{\vec{F}}_{\text{e}}}$ on a positive test charge. The SI unit of electric field is N/C. Here it should be noted that the test charge ${{\text{q}}_{0}}$ does not disturb other charges which produces $\vec{E}$. With the concept of electric field, our description of electric interactions has two parts. First, a given charge distribution acts as a source of electric field. Second, the electric field exerts a force on any charge that is present in this field.

Electric Field leads to a Force 

Suppose there is an electric field strength $\vec{E}$ at some point in an electric field, then the electrostatic force acting on a charge $+q$ is $qE$ in the direction of $\vec{E}$, while on the charge $-q$ it is $qE$ in the opposite direction of $\vec{E}$.

concepts.title: Electric Field Intensity Due to Point Charge
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Field intensity due to a Point Charge 

The force experienced by a unit positive charge at a distance $r$ due to point charge ' $q$ ' is called electric field intensity at that point due to the charge $q$.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{F}=\left( \frac{\text{K}{{\text{q}}_{1}}{{\text{q}}_{2}}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \right)\text{ where }\text{ K}=9~{{10}^{9}}~\text{N}{{\text{m}}^{2}}/{{\text{C}}^{2}} \\ \text{ and }\text{ K}=\left( \frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}} \right)\text{ where }{{\varepsilon }_{0}}=8.85{{10}^{-12}}{{~\text{C}}^{2}}/\text{N}{{\text{m}}^{2}} \\ \end{array}$

We define electric field at a point as

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{E}=\left( \frac{\text{KQ}.1}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{KQ}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \right) \\ {} & \text{E}=\frac{\text{KQ}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}\text{ or }\text{ E}=\frac{\text{Q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \\ \end{array}$

In vector form

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \vec{E}=\text{E}\cdot {{\overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,}_{\text{r}}} \\ {} & =\left( \frac{\text{KQ}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}} \right)\cdot {{\overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,}_{\text{r}}}=\left( \frac{\text{KQ}}{{{\text{r}}^{3}}} \right)\vec{r} \\ {} & \vec{E}=\left( \frac{\text{KQ}}{{{\text{r}}^{3}}} \right)\vec{r }\vec{r}=\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\text{i} \\ &+\left( {{\text{y}}_{2}}-{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)\text{j}+\left( {{\text{z}}_{2}}-{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)\text{k} \\ {} & \vec{E}\left. =\left( \frac{\text{Q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}{{\text{r}}^{3}}} \right)\vec{r }{{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}},{{\text{z}}_{2}} \right) \\ {} & {} \\ \end{array}$

$\therefore $Net field intensity in the space due to a number of point charges at a point is given by principle of superposition.

$\vec{E}={{\vec{E}}_{1}}+{{\vec{E}}_{2}}+{{\vec{E}}_{3}}+\ldots ..+{{\vec{E}}_{\text{n}}}$

[Principle of superposition is applicable only when the effect remains unchanged, due to presence of others (charges in this case).]

1 Net force experienced by a point charge in the electric field

$\vec{F}=\text{q}\vec{E}$

concepts.title: Due to Uniform Charged Circular Ring
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Potential due to Uniform Charged Circular Ring 

Consider an infinitesmal element of charge dq on the circumference of the ring, then the electric potential due to this charge is

$\text{dV}=\frac{\text{dq}}{4\pi {{\varepsilon }_{0}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{R}}^{2}}}}$

Integrating,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}{} & ~\therefore \text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{R}}^{2}}}}\int ~\text{dq} \\{} & {} \\{} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}=\frac{\text{Q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{R}}^{2}}}} \\\end{array}$

This result for potential remains same even if the charge distribution is non-uniform.

(ii) Electric field intensity at an axial point

Consider small elements of charge dq on the circumference Note that the net field will be in the axial direction, since the vertical components will cancel out.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d}{{\text{E}}_{\text{x}}}=\int \text{dEcos}\theta $

$d{{E}_{x}}=\frac{dq}{\left( 4\pi {{\in }_{0}} \right)\left( {{R}^{2}}+{{x}^{2}} \right)}\times \frac{x}{{{\left( {{R}^{2}}+{{x}^{2}} \right)}^{1/2}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left\{ \text{cos}\theta =\frac{x}{\sqrt{{{R}^{2}}+{{x}^{2}}}} \right\}$

 

$\text{E}=\frac{\text{Qx}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}{{\left( {{\text{R}}^{2}}+{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}$

2nd Method : Differentiate the result for the field intensity.

Potential at $\text{P}$ due to ring

$\text{V}=\frac{1}{4\pi {{\epsilon }_{0}}}\frac{\text{Q}}{\sqrt{{{\text{R}}^{2}}+{{\text{x}}^{2}}}}$

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{E}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{-\text{dV}}{\text{dx}}=\frac{-\text{Q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}}\left\{ \frac{-2\text{x}}{2{{\left( {{\text{R}}^{2}}+{{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)}^{3/2}}} \right\}=\frac{\text{Qx}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}{{\left( {{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{R}}^{2}} \right)}^{3/2}}}$

(shape change from

Convex to Concave)

Note : Maximum fied exists at $\left( \text{R}/\sqrt{2} \right)$ from the ring.

concepts.title: Due to Uniform Charged Disc
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Potential due to Uniform Charged Disc 

The disc has a charge density $\sigma $ (charge per unit area)

For analyzing a charged disc, consider a ring of thickness $\text{dx}$ at a distance $\text{x}$ from the centre; $\text{dq}=\sigma \left( 2\pi \text{x} \right)\text{dx}$ {charge on the ring of thickness $\text{dx}\}$

Now, Potential at $\text{P}$ due to the ring

$\text{dV}=\frac{\text{Kdq}}{\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{r}}^{2}}}}$

On integrating, we get the potential for entire disc.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}=\int _{0}^{\text{R}}\frac{\text{K}\sigma \left( 2\pi \text{x} \right)\text{dx}}{\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{r}}^{2}}}}$

$=\text{K}\sigma \left( 2\pi \right)\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{R}}\frac{\text{xdx}}{\sqrt{{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{r}}^{2}}}}$

$\text{V}=\text{K}\sigma 2\pi \left\{ \sqrt{{{\text{R}}^{2}}+{{\text{r}}^{2}}}-\left| \text{r} \right| \right\}$

Electric Field Intensity

Now, we know that

$\text{E}=\frac{-\text{dV}}{\text{dr}}=-\frac{\sigma }{2{{\epsilon }_{0}}}\left( \frac{2\text{r}}{2\sqrt{{{\text{R}}^{2}}+{{\text{r}}^{2}}}}-1 \right]$

$\text{E}=\frac{\sigma }{2{{\in }_{0}}}\left[ 1-\frac{\text{r}}{\sqrt{{{\text{R}}^{2}}+{{\text{r}}^{2}}}} \right]$

Plot of potential $\text{v}/\text{s}$ distance from the disc

Plot of field intensity v/s distance from the disc If $\text{r}\to 0,\text{E}=\frac{\sigma }{2{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

Thus, near a uniformly charged surface, $\text{E}$ can be taken $=\frac{\sigma }{2{{\epsilon }_{0}}}$

concepts.title: Due to Hollow Sphere
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Potential Due to Hollow Sphere 

For analyzing a charged shell, we consider a ring of thickness $\text{Rd}\theta $ at an angle of $\theta $ from the horizontal.

The shell has a charged density $\sigma $ and radius $R,\sigma =\frac{Q}{4\pi {{R}^{2}}}$

So, charge on a ring of thickness $\text{Rd}\theta $

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & dq=\sigma \left( 2\pi R\text{sin}\theta \right)Rd\theta \\ {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ dV}=\frac{\text{dq}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\text{l}} \\ {} & \text{dV}=\frac{\sigma \left( 2\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}}\text{sin}\theta \right)\text{d}\theta }{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\text{l}} \\ {} & ~=\frac{\text{KQ}}{\left( 4\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}} \right)}\left( 2\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}} \right)\left( \frac{\text{sin}\theta \text{d}\theta }{\text{l}} \right)=\frac{\text{KQ}}{2}\left( \frac{\text{sin}\theta \text{d}\theta }{\text{l}} \right) \\ {} & \text{cos}\theta =\frac{{{\text{r}}^{2}}+{{\text{R}}^{2}}-{{\text{l}}^{2}}}{2\text{rR}} \\ {} & 2rR\text{cos}\theta ={{\text{r}}^{2}}+{{\text{R}}^{2}}-{{\text{l}}^{2}} \\ {} & ~-2\text{rRsin}\theta \text{d}\theta =-2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d }\!\!~\!\!\text{ d} \\ \end{array}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{dl}=\text{rR}\left( \frac{\text{sin}\theta \text{d}\theta }{\text{l}} \right) \\ {} & \text{V}=\int ~\text{dV}=\frac{\text{KQ}}{2}\mathop{\int }_{\left( \text{r}-\text{R} \right)}^{\text{r}+\text{R}}\frac{\text{dl}}{\text{rR}}=\frac{\text{KQ}}{2\text{rR}}\left( \text{r}+\text{R}-\text{r}+\text{R} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{KQ}}{\text{r}} \right) \\ {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}=\left( \frac{\text{KQ}}{\text{r}} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ when }\!\!~\!\!\text{ r}>\text{R} \\ {} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ r}<\text{R} \\ {} & \text{V}=\int ~\text{dV}=\mathop{\int }_{\left( \text{R}-\text{r} \right)}^{\left( \text{R}+\text{r} \right)}\frac{\left( \text{KQ} \right)\text{dl}}{2\text{rR}}=\left( \frac{\text{KQ}}{\text{R}} \right),\text{r}<\text{R} \\ \end{array}$

Summarizing (Uniformly charged shell)

(i) For any external part $(r>R)$

(a) Electric potential

$=\frac{\text{Q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\text{r}}$

(b) Electric field $=\frac{-\text{dV}}{\text{dr}}=\frac{\text{Q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}{{\text{r}}^{2}}}$

Note : For an external point a shell has similar formulae as a point charge hence this point can be very useful for some objective questions.

(ii) For any internal point $(r<\mathbf{R})$

(a) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Electric potential $=\frac{\text{Q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\text{R}}$

(b) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Electric field $=\frac{-dV}{dr}=0$ 
 

Plot of potential v/s distance from the centre of shell and Plot of electric field intensity $\text{V}/\text{s}$ distance from the centre of shell.

concepts.title: Due to Solid Sphere
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric Potential Due to Solid Sphere

To calculate electric potential at a point inside the sphere, we divide the entire spherical volume into two spherical parts I and II i.e. the region containing charge outside the point and the region inside the point.

The sphere has a charge density

$\rho $ (charge per unit volume)

$\rho =\frac{\text{Q}}{\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{3}}}$

Now, consider region II

$\text{d}{{\text{V}}_{1}}=\text{K}\left( \frac{\text{dq}}{\text{r}} \right)$

{Using the formula for potential of external point for concentric shell}

${{\text{V}}_{1}}=\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{r}}\text{K}\frac{\text{dq}}{\text{r}},\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{r}}\text{dq}=\rho \left( \frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{r}}^{3}} \right)$ 

$\rho =\frac{\text{Q}}{\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{3}}}$

$\Rightarrow {{\text{V}}_{1}}=\frac{\text{KQ}{{\text{r}}^{2}}}{{{\text{R}}^{3}}}$

Consider Region I 

$\therefore \text{d}{{\text{V}}_{2}}=\text{K}\frac{\text{dq}}{\text{x}},\text{dq}=\rho \left( 4\pi {{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)\text{dx}$

$\Rightarrow {{\text{V}}_{2}}=\text{K}\rho \left( 4\pi \right)\int _{\text{r}}^{\text{R}}\text{x}d\text{x}$

$=4\pi \rho \text{K}\left( \frac{{{\text{R}}^{2}}-{{\text{r}}^{2}}}{2} \right)$

$=\frac{4\pi \text{QK}}{\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{3}}}\left( \frac{{{\text{R}}^{2}}-{{\text{r}}^{2}}}{2} \right)$

${{\text{V}}_{2}}=\frac{3\text{KQ}}{2{{\text{R}}^{3}}}\left( {{\text{R}}^{2}}-{{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)$

Hence, net potential $\left( {{\text{V}}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}} \right)$

${{\text{V}}_{\text{net }}}={{\text{V}}_{1}}+{{\text{V}}_{2}}=\frac{\text{KQ}{{\text{r}}^{2}}}{{{\text{R}}^{3}}}+\frac{3\text{KQ}}{2{{\text{R}}^{3}}}\left( {{\text{R}}^{2}}-{{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)=\frac{\text{KQ}}{2{{\text{R}}^{3}}}\left( 3{{\text{R}}^{2}}-{{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)$

Summarizing for a uniformly charged sphere :

(i) For an internal point $(r<R)$

Net potential inside the sphere $=\frac{Q\left( 3{{R}^{2}}-{{r}^{2}} \right)}{4\pi {{\in }_{0}}\left( 2{{R}^{3}} \right)}$

Net field inside the sphere

$\text{E}=-\frac{\text{dV}}{\text{dr}}=\frac{2\text{Qr}}{4\pi {{\in }_{\text{O}}}\left( 2{{\text{R}}^{3}} \right)}=\frac{\rho \text{r}}{3{{\in }_{\text{O}}}}$

This formula is of importance and can be noted in a vector form as well i.e.

$\vec{E}=\frac{\rho \vec{r}}{3{{\epsilon }_{\text{O}}}}$

(ii) $r>\mathbf{R}$

Net potential outside the sphere

$=\frac{\text{K}}{\text{r}}\int ~\text{dq}=\left( \frac{\text{KQ}}{\text{r}} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{Q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{\text{O}}}\text{r}} \right)$

Net field $=-\left( \frac{\text{dV}}{\text{dr}} \right)=\frac{\text{Q}}{4\pi {{\in }_{\text{O}}}{{\text{r}}^{2}}}$

Note : A uniformly charged sphere also behave as a point charge for any external point.

concepts.title: Eectric field in a cavity
concepts.conceptBody:
Electric field in a cavity

Example:

Find the field intensity in the region of intersection of two uniformly charged sphere with volume charge density $+\rho $ and $-\rho $. The distance between centres is 1 .

Solution:

At the point $\text{P}$, electric field due to sphere $\text{O}$ and $\text{O'}$ can be calculated and added vectorially

${\vec{E}_{\text{O}}}=\frac{\rho \overrightarrow{{{\text{r}}_{1}}}}{3{{\in }_{\text{O}}}},{\vec{E}_{{\text{O'}}}}=\frac{\left( -\rho \right)\overrightarrow{{{\text{r}}_{2}}}}{3{{\in }_{\text{O}}}}$

$\vec{E}_{\text{net}}=\vec{E}={\vec{E}_{\text{O}}}+{\vec{E}_{\text{O}}}$

$\therefore \vec{E}=\left(\frac{\rho \overrightarrow{{{\text{r}}_{1}}}}{3{{\in }_{\text{O}}}} \right)-\left(\frac{\rho \overrightarrow{{{\text{r}}_{2}}}}{3{{\in }_{\text{O}}}} \right)=\frac{\rho }{3{\in }_{\text{O}}}\left({{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}-{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}} \right)=\frac{\rho }{3{{\in }_{\text{O}}}}O{O}'=\left(\frac{\rho \vec{l}}{3{{\in }_{\text{O}}}} \right)$

Remark : 

(i) Thus, it can be easily seen that the field intensity comes out to be consistent inside the common area and is directed from positive to negative centres of the sphere.

(ii) Here, $\rho $ and $-\rho $ must be same.

Example:

Cavity (it is a special case of previous question). 

A sphere with centre $O$ is filled with a charge density $+\rho $. There exists a cavity inside this sphere which is also a spherical cavity with centre ${O}'$ at a distance $\vec{l}$ from the centre $O$. Find the electric field strength at a point $\text{P}$ inside the cavity ?

Solution : 

Concept : Fill the cavity with charge densities $+\rho $ and $-\rho $ so that the cavity is filled.

This makes it a special case of previous example where the common volume between the 2 spheres is same as one of the sphere.

Hence, ${{\vec{E}}_{0}}=\frac{\rho {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}}{3{{\in }_{0}}},{{\vec{E}}_{{{0}'}}}=\frac{\left( -\rho \right){{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}}{3{{\in }_{0}}}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {\vec{E}} ~={{{\vec{E}}}_{0}}-{{{\vec{E}}}_{{{0}'}}}=\frac{\rho \left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}-{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}} \right)}{3{{\epsilon }_{0}}} \\ {} ~=\frac{\rho \vec{l}}{3{{\epsilon }_{0}}}\left\{ \because {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}-{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}=\vec{l} \right\} \\ \end{array}$
